description_long: This is a military twin-engine propeller airplane with a light brown fuselage and broad, straight wings featuring U.S. Air Force insignia. The aircraft has a glazed nose section, likely for a bombardier or navigator, and a cockpit positioned just behind the nose with multiple windows for visibility. The engines are mounted on the wings, each with a three-bladed propeller, and the landing gear is extended, showing a tricycle configuration. The tail section features a twin vertical stabilizer (H-tail) connected by a horizontal stabilizer. The surface appears metallic with some weathering and paint chipping, giving it a rugged, operational look. The overall design suggests a World War II-era medium bomber or reconnaissance aircraft.
description: A twin-engine, light brown military propeller airplane with U.S. Air Force markings, glazed nose, and twin tail fins. Features include a tricycle landing gear, broad straight wings, and a weathered metallic surface.
description_view_0: Front view showing the glazed nose, cockpit windows, twin propellers, and the symmetrical twin tail fins.
description_view_1: Left-front oblique view highlighting the left wing insignia, engine nacelle, and the extended landing gear.
description_view_2: Rear view displaying the twin vertical stabilizers, horizontal stabilizer, and the full wingspan.
description_view_3: Right-front oblique view showing the right wing insignia, propeller, and the cockpit's side windows.
attribute_wingspan_configuration: Mid-wing, straight wings with no visible winglets or pronounced dihedral/anhedral angles.
attribute_engine_type: Twin piston engines mounted on the wings, each with a three-bladed propeller.
attribute_tail_design: H-tail configuration with twin vertical stabilizers connected by a horizontal stabilizer.
attribute_landing_gear_type: Tricycle landing gear with two main gear units under the wings and a single nose gear, all extended.
attribute_cockpit_placement: Forward-mounted, enclosed cockpit with multiple windows, positioned just behind the glazed nose section, likely multi-seat.
